predicted.recommended_action: Request explicit written confirmation from the intended owner before treating this as committed work.

actual_outcome.description: The team lead (Sarah) had posted the Loom video with the message: 'Please 👍 if you can commit to delivering this by May 20.' All 4 👍 reactions were from team members who have historically treated this emoji protocol as binding commitment. Sarah proceeded to plan sprint capacity assuming the commitment was real. The agent's recommendation to 'request explicit written confirmation' was seen as bureaucratic overhead and ignored. The work was delivered on time.

evidence.summary: Team charter explicitly states: 'In async Loom reviews, 👍 on a deliverable-bound message = commitment. 👀 = reviewing. ❓ = blocking concern.'

Sarah confirmed: 'We have used this protocol for 8 months. No one has ever 👍 and then not delivered. The agent treating it as unresolved made me question whether KDNA understands modern async workflows.'

impact.qualitative_impact: Erosion of trust in agent judgment. User explicitly questioned whether KDNA understands modern async workflows. If this pattern repeats, user may stop loading decision_state for async reviews.

feedback.notes: This is not a one-off. Async teams increasingly use emoji protocols, structured comments, and tool-native reactions as commitment mechanisms. The axiom ax-absence-not-commitment is too absolute. It needs a boundary condition for teams that have explicit async commitment protocols.

suggested_changes.change: Add does_not_apply_when entry for teams with documented async commitment protocols (emoji-based, structured reaction systems, or tool-native approval flows where the team charter explicitly defines what constitutes commitment).
